Your May spending tells a clear story — $9,256 in total expenses with some genuinely strong financial habits alongside a few areas worth a closer look.
The good news first: you're contributing $2,000/month to retirement accounts (Salesforce and UCHealth 401(k)s), $400 to your HSA, and $300 to a Colorado 529 — these are productive "outflows" that are building long-term wealth, not waste.
That said, here are the areas most likely representing real inefficiency or optimization opportunities:
Backup childcare: a $220 variable cost worth watching
The Care.com backup care charge ($220 for a single day) stands out as a high per-unit cost. If this is a recurring need, it may be worth exploring a monthly backup care contract or checking whether your employer offers dependent care FSA dollars or subsidized backup care programs — many large employers like Salesforce do. Over 12 months, even two backup days per month adds up to $5,280.
Groceries: $1,024 for one month
Between Costco ($737 across two trips) and King Soopers ($287), your grocery bill is substantial. This isn't necessarily wasteful — Costco in particular tends to reflect bulk buying that saves money per unit. The question worth asking is whether there's food waste happening at home, or whether the Costco quantities are genuinely being used.
Streaming subscriptions: small but easy to trim
Spotify Family ($20) and Disney Plus ($16) total $36/month — $432/year. These are modest, but if either isn't getting regular use, it's the simplest possible win. Worth a quick audit.
Insurance: worth an annual rate check
State Farm home ($154) and auto ($218) together are $372/month — $4,464/year. Insurance premiums tend to drift upward quietly. Getting competing quotes annually (especially if your driving record or home profile has improved) often surfaces $200–$600/year in savings without changing coverage.
What's notably not a problem
Your mortgage ($3,850) is your largest line item at 42% of May spending, but that's a fixed obligation and likely appropriate given Denver home values. Your drinks & dining ($165) and personal care ($44) are quite restrained relative to your income level.
The honest assessment: your spending is well-structured. The biggest lever isn't cutting lifestyle — it's optimizing the backup childcare cost and doing an annual insurance review.
Would you like to dig into what your recurring monthly obligations look like going forward, to see if there are any bills due soon worth reviewing?
